data.title: ONEOK: Fee-Based Stability Amid AI Frenzy
data.description:
My Thesis

As the AI craze continues, I am seeking high-quality opportunities to expand the weight of my defensive stocks. Readers who have followed me for a long time know that I like investing in large midstream companies because of their financial stability and lower dependence of their earnings on the prices of hydrocarbons. As I plan to expand part of my portfolio invested in midstream assets, I want to diversify and invest in a new company for me, which is ONEOK, Inc. (NYSE:OKE). It is not as popular and large as industry leaders like Enbridge or Energy Transfer, but it still offers a compelling forward dividend yield of 6% and solid upside potential. Natural gas exposure is solid, which is crucial because of the expected crucial role of this fuel type to drive AI innovation. Capital allocation looks sound, meaning that OKE will highly likely be able to balance generous dividend payouts together with investments in future growth. All in all, I initiate OKE coverage with a "Strong Buy" rating.

OKE Analysis

Since it is my first coverage of ONEOK, I start with a brief description of the company's business. According to the FY2024 10-K report, OKE is a "leading midstream service provider of gathering, processing, fractionation, transportation, storage and marine export services". The management says that 90% of the company's consolidated earnings were fee-based in 2024. It is very good because it means limited dependence on highly volatile commodity prices. ONEOK operates via four segments, which are:

  • Natural Gas Gathering and Processing;
  • Natural Gas Liquids [NGL];
  • Natural Gas Pipelines; and
  • Refined Products and Crude.

The business mix looks robust because it is not about all eggs in the same basket in ONEOK's case. Yes, the NGL segment is by far the largest one both in terms of revenue and adjusted EBITDA. However, the Natural Gas Gathering and Processing segment as well as the Refined Products and Crude Segments are also the ones that generate billions per year both in revenue and EBITDA. The company boasts a 60,000-mile pipeline network and loads of other facilities strategically positioned in America's most prolific oil and gas basins. It is not easy to replicate such a vast infrastructure, which ensures OKE's wide strategic moat. Therefore, we see that OKE's strategic positioning is strong. Moreover, the company is rebalancing its portfolio of assets to maximize value for shareholders.

And the management converts this strong strategic positioning into financial excellence. OKE demonstrated double-digit CAGR over the last decade across the board of the most important profitability metrics. I also like that OKE's profitability ratios outpaced revenue growth over the last decade. To me it certainly indicates the management's value to maximize value for shareholders via cost efficiency.

The upcoming quarter's earnings are scheduled for October 28 post-market. OKE's Q3 revenue is expected to be $8.23 billion, which will be higher by 4% if compared on a QoQ basis. The best part is that the company's bottom line is expected to outpace revenue growth with a projected 7% QoQ increase, from $1.34 to $1.44. I am not comparing on a YoY basis because there will be huge percentage changes due to the effect of M&A spree during late 2024 and early 2025. But the main point here is that OKE continues demonstrating revenue growth and operating leverage, which are the most important criteria for me as a value investor.

Another crucial point for any value investor is a company's financial position. Midstream expansion projects are costly and usually have long durations, meaning that companies are raising large amounts via debt financing. Therefore, I am not surprised by OKE's large $32.5 billion total debt. With the company's strong FCF generating capacity, the covered ratio sits at a comfortable level of 3.3. Moreover, OKE's total debt is significantly lower compared to the market cap, meaning there is solid room for debt restructuring if needed.

I expect the company to maintain its robust balance sheet over the long term and the management to remain confident in its FY2025 outlook. Despite volatility in oil prices, OKE is likely to benefit from broader favorable trends like the current global data center spending spree as well as the strategic priority of President Trump's administration to fortify the manufacturing potential of the country. Both data centers and new factories will need uninterrupted energy supply, and a player with an extensive midstream ecosystem will certainly be there to ensure that energy transfers from oil and gas basins to end customers.

Valuation

To value a dividend machine like OKE, I usually prefer the approach called the dividend discount model [DDM]. The discount rate is 7.54%, which is the company's cost of equity calculated using the CAPM approach.

Wall Street analysts forecast OKE's FY2026 dividend to be $4.30. To be conservative, a 2% perpetual growth rate was used, which aligns with the Fed's inflation target.

According to my calculations, OKE's fair share price is almost $78. The last close is around $69, meaning that there is an attractive 13% upside potential. The upside looks especially good if you also keep in mind OKE's 6% forward dividend yield.

Wall Street analysts are even more optimistic regarding the fair share price is their average target price is far above my estimates. The consensus target price of $92.5 means that Wall Street analysts believe that the stock is 34% undervalued. From the forward P/E ratios forecast's perspective, OKE is also extremely undervalued with the expected decline of P/E from around 13 to below 10 over the next few years.

Risks To Consider

The business faces significant operational risks that might lead to material, unfavorable financial and reputational consequences. Dangers include pipeline leaks, equipment failures, and disruptions due to weather events or natural disasters.

As I mentioned at the beginning of my analysis, OKE's revenues are mostly fee-based, meaning protection from volatility in crude oil or natural gas prices. However, they are vulnerable to unfavorable changes in the energy supply-demand equilibrium. Weaker production activity in the energy upstream sector will inevitably lead to less throughput volumes on OKE's systems.

Now let's also mention the risks related to the upcoming earnings release. ONEOK's earnings surprise record is quite far from perfect with six negative revenue and five EPS misses over the last eight quarters. When a company delivers a solid double beat against consensus forecasts, there is a quite high probability of the share price rising significantly because of the growing investors' confidence. And vice versa, when the business struggles to consistently surpass consensus estimates, investors' confidence melts down. In case of a new double miss, the Q3 earnings release might become a negative stock price catalyst.

Bottom Line

To conclude, OKE is a "Strong Buy". I choose the stock of its quality and strategic positioning in the rock-solid midstream industry of the United States. Compelling dividend yield and notable undervaluation are likely to protect me in case of the broader stock market's deteriorating sentiment.

data.title: Crispr And Intellia: Advancing In Vivo Gene Editing As 2025 Results Strengthen Investment Outlook
data.description:
Background

My last coverage of Crispr Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CRSP) and Intellia Therapeutics (NASDAQ:NTLA) was published in May 2025. At the time, gene therapy stocks were suffering from regulatory uncertainties, potential pricing regulations in the U.S., and distrust given rising safety and commercialisation concerns. As stated in my previous article, the pipeline and financial stability of both stocks supported a bullish case for CRSP and NTLA, which were trading at $38.25 and $8.84, respectively. Although the uncertainties surrounding gene therapy stocks are not completely gone, in the recent months the market has been getting more optimistic about the therapeutic potential of in vivo gene therapies, and therefore its commercialisation potential. This market optimism triggered both stocks to outperform when compared against SP500’s performance in the last six-months (see image below).

NTLA had the best performance, rewarding investors with 241% total returns in the last six-months. Given that Intellia is still in a pre-commercialisation stage, expecting to reach commercial stage in 2027, its investment thesis has a higher risk profile than the investment in CRSP, which has a larger cash runway, a diversified pipeline, and a commercial product (CASGEVY). Demonstrating that sometimes investing in companies with a high-risk value translates into high returns. But, past performance does not guarantee future results.

The current analysis will focus on the in vivo gene therapies developed by CRSP and NTLA, as in my opinion those product candidates have the largest potential to boost investment returns.

Thesis

Crispr is expected to release the Phase I clinical trial results from CTX310 before yearend. CTX310 is an in vivo single dose gene therapy aiming to delete angiopoietin-like 3 (ANGPTL3) for the treatment of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). The company is estimating that in the U.S. alone, there are more than 40 million patients that could benefit from CTX310 if/when commercially available. ASCVD’s total addressable market in 2024 was estimated at $24 billion across the seven largest markets, and it is projected to reach up to $32.3 billion by 2035. Beyond this product candidate, CASGEVY is projected to deliver increasing revenue, which is expected to push Crispr’s balance sheet to break even from 2026. While other promising product candidates including allogeneic CAR-Ts targeting cancer and autoimmune disease complete a diverse pipeline that support a bullish case for long-term investors. However, taking in consideration the current valuation, and the binary nature of CTX310 upcoming clinical trial results, I downgrade my rating for CRSP from “Strong Buy” to a “Hold” as I believe the share price is already pricing in the potential success of CTX310 in Phase I trials.

Intellia, on the other hand, has a smaller pipeline solely focused on in vivo gene therapies. The company is expecting to file for regulatory approval of Lonvo-z (previously known as NTLA-2002) in the second half of 2026. If approved, Lonvo-z will become Intellia’s first commercial asset. This product candidate is aimed as a single dose in vivo gene therapy for the treatment of Hereditary Angioedema (HAE). Recently, Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS) gained FDA approval for Dawnzera, an RNA-based treatment against HAE. In contrast with Lonvo-z, which is a single dose gene therapy, Ionis’ RNA-based treatment was approved as once every four and once every eight weeks prophylactic therapy against HAE. Since Dawnzera’s approval, IONS share price rose 67%, currently trading at $71/sh. Although Lonvo-z may have the therapeutic advantage against Dawnzera, as a single dose treatment, its potential approval is forecasted for H1 2027 at the earliest. The HAE total addressable market in 2025 was estimated at $3.13 billion, and it is expected to be valued at $5.96 billion by 2032. Taking in consideration the low competitive landscape in HAE, Lonvo-z’s potential regulatory success is poised to boost NTLA’s share price in the next twelve months. Thus, I reiterate my “Buy” rating for long-term investors with a high risk tolerance, while recommending caution and diversification given the binary nature of such an investment thesis based on potential regulatory outcomes.

In vivo gene editing

The field of gene therapies is currently evolving towards a very exciting future. The treatments based on gene therapies (including gene editing, RNA-based, DNA-based) were focused on rare conditions, partially because the patients suffering from those conditions have a lack of efficient therapeutic options, thus justifying the development of precision medicines. Two very well-known examples are CASGEVY and ELEVIDYS, for the treatment of sickle-cell disease and Duchenne muscular dystrophy, respectively. However, some biotech and pharmaceutical companies are moving towards the development of gene therapies targeting conditions with a larger patient population, for instance Voyager Therapeutics (VYGR) has gene therapy product candidates targeting Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, among others. This technology is currently being developed for the treatment of cancer, autoimmune disease, neurodegenerative and cardiovascular conditions.

In the area of cardiovascular, the product candidates from Crispr and Intellia are particularly exciting given their use of non-viral vectors with high tissue specificity and their capacity to be therapeutically efficient after a single intravenous dose. If successful in clinical trials through regulatory approval, I believe this technology could be transformative in the treatment of cardiovascular conditions, which remain one of the largest causes of death worldwide.

Crispr Therapeutics

Crispr accounts with four gene editing in vivo programs, all of them aimed to be delivered via intravenous systemic injection. The product candidates consist of a lipid nanoparticle that encapsulates a Cas9 mRNA with a guide RNA targeting the gene of interest. The two most advanced programs are CTX310 and CTX320, which are in Phase I clinical trials (see image below).

CTX310 and CTX320 are targeting indications in ASCVD. CTX310 is expected to deliver Phase I results before the year-end. To date, the preliminary results have been very promising, showing over 81% reduction in triglycerides and up to 64.6% reduction in LDL cholesterol after 30 days of treatment (see image below), without presenting any safety or tolerability concerns.

Given the impressive topline results, the market is counting with positive Phase I results, supporting the progression of this product candidate towards Phase II. However, CTX310’s potential positive results are likely to validate Crispr’s in vivo gene editing platform, which includes CTX320, CTX340 and CTX450. In my previous analysis, I highlighted the therapeutic and financial advantages of the in vivo gene editing platforms against the ex vivo therapies, i.e. CASGEVY. Briefly, the in vivo gene editing platform offers the opportunity to be manufactured in bulk, to be delivered to the patient as off-the-shelf treatment, in a safe and timely manner, while also reducing COGS significantly, and therefore increasing the profit margins. Furthermore, Crispr’s in vivo gene editing platform is targeting indications with large patient populations with the potential to become large revenue makers if/when they gain regulatory approval.

Intellia Therapeutics

Intellia’s whole pipeline is focused on two single-dose in vivo gene editing product candidates. Lonvo-z against HAE and Nex-z for the treatment of Transthyretin Amyloidosis (ATTR). The first one is expected to become commercially available by 2027, pending regulatory approval. While Nex-z is projected to reach commercial stage by 2028 (see image below).

Based on Intellia’s projections, both product candidates have blockbuster potential by 2028. Likewise, Crispr’s in vivo gene editing candidates, Intellia’s candidates utilise lipid nanoparticles to encapsulate the gene editing molecules. The use of lipid nanoparticles with specific targeting not only promote the efficient intracellular delivery of therapeutic molecules, but also enable the scalability of manufacturing procedures that in turn reduces COGS, when compared against viral delivery, and facilitates the stock availability to cover the potential commercial demand.

Lonvo-z’s phase II long-term results revealed that 98% of patients treated with the product candidates were free from HEA attacks after 3 years of infusion. While 73% of them were also free from using any other treatment to control their condition. The strong phase II clinical trial performance resulted in a positive opinion from prescribers that supports strong commercial potential of the treatment if/when it becomes commercially available (see image below).

Financials

Crispr Therapeutics and Intellia Therapeutics are due to release Q3 2025 financial results on November 6th. Generally speaking, I am not expecting any major surprises from the financial point of view, although Crispr has a commercially available product, the management seems to be waiting for the Q4 2025 report to release CASGEVY’s full year revenue performance, while their large cash availability allows them to fund operations, including a diverse pipeline in various development stages. On the other hand, Intellia remains in clinical-stage, while their cash availability is significantly smaller when compared against Crispr’s, their pipeline is focused on two assets with the potential to become commercially available in 2027 and 2028. The following table summarises the financial performance of both companies as stated in their H1 2025 results.

Despite holding over $1.7 billion, last week, Crispr filed a $600 million stock offer which aims to secure cash at the company’s discretion. Crispr’s management is avoiding providing specific guidance associated with CASGEVY’s full year sales revenue. However, in its quarterly reports, and recent conference presentations the management sounds confident about CASGEVY’s capacity to drive the company towards profitability by 2028, while supporting the development of multiple promising assets, including the in vivo gene editing platform. In terms of CASGEVY roll-out, Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), who is CASGEVY’s co-developer, has already activated over 75 authorised treatment centres worldwide, while over 115 patients have performed the cell collection stage associated with the treatment. For more details, check out my first analysis of Crispr where I described CASGEVY’s treatment journey. Overall, if I estimate Crispr’s product revenue as zero, the cash on hand ($1.721 billion) equates to a cash runway of 2.2 years at an average quarterly burn rate of $189.5 million. This cash runway will enable Crispr to advance several of their product candidates, including the allogeneic CAR-Ts and in vivo gene editing candidates, which are all aimed as off-the-shelf products, with lower COGS and higher profit margins when compared against CASGEVY. Thus, from the financial point of view, the company is very healthy and able to support its promising pipeline.

On the other hand, Intellia is being very open with its revenue expectations, presuming regulatory approval, they expect their assets to be commercially successful from the first year of launch. Considering that the in vivo gene editing therapies are aimed as off-the-shelf products which have a reduced manufacturing complexity, and lower COGS when compared against ex-vivo therapies, and more importantly the positive opinion of prescribers who appreciate the therapeutic advantage of Lonvo-z and Nex-z, I agree with Intellia’s optimistic revenue guidance from 2027 onwards. The company accounts for $459 million in cash, which considering the average quarterly burn rate in H1 2025 ($130.5 million) equates to 3.5 quarters cash runway (approximately Q4 2026). Nonetheless, Intellia is estimating its cash position is sufficient to cover operations until early 2027, which might be possible presuming they will reduce operating expenses when approaching Lonvo-z clinical trial completion. In this sense, NTLA’s financial position is relatively stable but dependent on clinical and regulatory success of their product candidates.

Valuation

CRSP is currently trading at $67.21 per share, and $6.11 billion market cap. Generally speaking, this year, Crispr has provided significant advances in its pipeline, taking advantage of their financial stability to fund the development of the next generation of assets. In the mid- to long-term, Crispr has the potential to follow the steps of their partner Vertex, in becoming an established profitable biotech/biopharmaceutical company with high quality therapeutic assets. Hence, I believe Crispr will be able to achieve profitability by 2028 at the latest, primarily supported by CASGEVY but being closely followed by other products, including those derived from the in vivo gene editing platform. Despite my long-term bullish sentiment, in this article I am providing a “Hold” rating on CRSP, a rating downgrade from “Strong Buy” six months ago. The main rationale behind the downgrade is that I believe the market has already priced in the potential Phase I clinical success of CTX310. Currently, Wall Street analysts are still given a Buy rating to CRSP, with an average share price target at $82.95. Meanwhile, the Seeking Alpha analyst consensus and Quant rating are rating the stock with a hold. Overall, the EPS growth is expected to be negative in 2025, while observing moderate growth in 2026 onwards, which supports my cautious assessment at this moment in time. Furthermore, insider trades are revealing the recent sell of common stock.

Intellia’s shares are trading at $25.89, and a $2.78 billion market cap. The company has gone through a series of changes this year that have secured their financial stability and business competitiveness. Lonvo-z and Nex-z clinical progress has helped Intellia to regain the market’s trust and positive opinion. For instance, Cathie Wood’s ARK fund has recently increased their position in NTLA and CRSP, while Citizens JMP raised their NTLA’s price target to $33/sh based on their HAE product candidate. This year, the consensus projects the EPS to grow by 21%, while observing a 3.44% growth in 2026 and 61% EPS growth in 2027. Projections based on Lonvo-z’s positive clinical and commercial outcome. In terms of analyst sentiment, NTLA is holding buy ratings from Wall Street ($34 average share price target), Seeking Alpha analysts and the Quant rating. Based on the clinical data released to date, I am optimistic about Lonvo-z potential to achieve commercial status by 2027, while the high clinical need and low competitive pressure supports the revenue projections suggesting commercial success from the first year of launch. In this sense, I reiterate NTLA’s moderate “Buy” rating for long-term investors with a high risk tolerant portfolio, while recommending implementing risk mitigating strategies such as diversification.

Risks

CRSP and NTLA have observed significant share price increases year-to-date, while their business and clinical performance supports the market optimism, it is also true that their investment thesis is based on speculative assumptions associated with the clinical and commercial success of high-risk product candidates.

In my opinion, Crispr, at this moment in time, is less risky than NTLA as an investment opportunity. But, NTLA’s mature in vivo gene editing product candidates and low competitive landscape has a larger potential to boost investor’s returns faster. Hence, my reiterated buy rating. However, investors interested in NTLA must consider the high risk of such speculative investment thesis based on potential clinical and commercial performance. Although the Phase Lonvo-z’ Phase II results were pretty impressive, given the relatively low number of participants, it is possible that the results do not translate into clinical trial success at Phase III.

Furthermore, the multiple regulatory and macroeconomic headwinds highlighted in my previous article focused on CRSP and NTLA are still considerable risks that may have a negative impact on profitability and share price of several biopharmaceutical and biotech stocks, including Crispr and Intellia.

Conclusions

In summary, 2025 has been a successful year for Crispr and Intellia. Both companies have sailed through multiple headwinds, while still supporting the development of product candidates with the potential, e.g. in vivo gene editing candidates, to become blockbusters in the next 5 years. The following table provides a summary of the upcoming catalysts of both companies (See image below)

In my opinion, Crispr is on a positive trajectory that supports their evolution towards becoming profitable by 2028 or earlier. However, in the short-term, I believe the share price already reflects the potential positive performance of CTX310 and the successful pipeline developments achieved in 2025. Thus, supporting my temporary “Hold” rating.

Likewise, Intellia has observed a sharp share price increase in the last six months. Mostly supported by pipeline developments, as well as positive sentiment about the company’s business decisions earlier this year. Intellia accounts for enough cash and equivalents to support operations until observing the clinical outcome of Lonvo-z in HEA. If the asset achieves clinical and regulatory success, Intellia’s shares are likely to observe a sharp increase similar to the one observed by IONS. However, the opposite scenario may cause a significant sell-off. While I reiterate my “Buy” rating for NTLA. Should investors start or increase positions in NTLA, they should also consider the binary nature of such speculative investment thesis and apply risk mitigating strategies such as diversification.

data.title: Molina Healthcare: Looks Like A Sick Puppy
data.description:
Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) is an upstart in the Health Insurance space. I last wrote about Molina in an article comparing them to United Healthcare (UNH) the old dog in the space.

At the time, I concluded that Molina probably offered the better valuation of the pair, and faced similar risks, however without the scale benefits of UNH.

Molina came out with earnings on October 22nd, and the results were terrible.

The company slashed guidance, cutting EPS estimates for the current year from $19 to $14. The problem was continued medical costs inflation. While much of the weakness in the Marketplace segment, this only accounted for half of the deterioration.

The continued slide in margins significantly reduces the credibility of the Molina outlook.

The market seems to agree with this. As at the time of writing, the Molina share price had dropped over 15%. Peer Centene (CNC) had dropped in sympathy by 4%. Elevance (ELV) had fared better, having reported Q3 already, while United Healthcare (UNH), also due to report, escaped pretty much unscathed.

Q3 Earnings and Outlook

Some level of negative response was inevitable following the big haircut in guidance. The quarter had some ominous figures.

Premium revenues were up around 11% year on year. However earnings per share shrunk to $1,51 compared to $5.65 for the comparable period, on a net income of $79m, down from $326m year on year.

The big driver of the miss was the medical care ratio, which came in at 92.6%, which shaved the after tax margin to a barely profitable 0.7% of revenues.

The company reported pressure in the medical care ratio in all segments, while pointing out that half of the deterioration came from the Marketplace segment, which accounted for just 10% of revenues. The MCR shift was dramatic from 85.4% to 95.6% for this segment.

This seems to be an attempt to pitch the problem as more easily addressable, by strong action in that one segment. However, the broad nature of the declines hints at a more pronounced utilisation, medical inflation, and pricing problem.

Of further concern is a negative cash flow print. Operating cash flow for the year to date was a negative of $237 million in contrast to the strong cash flow generation of $868m for the first 9 months of 2025. This is not due to cash being invested, but rather normal claims and Medicaid risk payments.

Investors would have hoped that the challenges had been fully recognised in Q2, when the previous earnings guidance was set due to the medical cost ratio coming in at 90.4%.

A full 2.2% worsening of the medical claims ratio points to real problems with the prior reserving assumptions.

At least Molina seems to be addressing the under-pricing in marketplace actively. This was discussed in an intense earnings call.

2026 rate increases averaged 30%, ranging from 15% to 45%. The company exited the most problematic counties, reducing the footprint by 20%. They expect premiums to reduce by half in 2026 as they cut exposure.

This provides a positive outlook, and management seemed confident of their $46bn premium projection for 2026, however, they did not provide detailed guidance on their expected medical cost ratio. They indicated that they hoped to bring the segment back to break-even.

The challenges in the Medicaid segment remain, with the medical cost ratio here still elevated. In my view, there was not enough detail given on management actions here to be confident that margins can be recovered.

Valuation

Valuations for the group are divided quite cleanly between the “Big Dogs” United and Elevance, and the “Pups” Molina and Centene.

Molina is the cheapest of the pack on a trailing price to earnings basis, sitting just under Centene at around 8x earnings.

Conclusion

The second difficult quarter in a row raises some serious questions about the persistent claims ratio creep. While price increases can be expected to help, this will be a drag on membership growth. A further concern is that the quality of the membership will be skewed by higher prices towards less healthy members. In this case price increases might not be effective in bringing down the medical cost ratio, and thus prove counter-productive to both top and bottom lines.

I am rating Molina as a hold until they are able to report a stabilised medical cost ratio.

Finally, the trends reported by Molina show a sharp decline in profitability. Looking forward to the upcoming earnings for UNH and CNC, investors should be cautious of similar trends pulling down their results. UNH has the buffer of PBM earnings, while CNC is more heavily exposed to insurance risk business. I’m taking a conservative position on UNH and particularly CNC going into earnings.

data.title: SCHG: A Simple, Low Cost Fund For Big Growth & Long-Term Power
data.description:
Introduction

In times like now, when the market continues to make new highs despite macro uncertainty, investors may be wondering where to invest their money. Although an overvalued market is risky due to a potential crash or correction, investing in index funds is key for long-term foundational growth.

As a simple guy like Warren Buffett, the Oracle of Omaha, has always recommended investing in an index fund and holding for the long-term. And Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHG) is a perfect example.

This is because of their passive investment style and low cost with an expense ratio of just 0.04%. This means you only pay $4 annually for every $10,000 invested, something important if you plan on holding for the foreseeable future.

Moreover, SCHG is also a way to get exposure to Technology (XLK) giants, many whom are overvalued due to the explosive growth of Artificial Intelligence. If you're looking for a low-cost fund for big growth and to build your wealth, consider SCHG.

Previous Buy Case

I last covered SCHG back in July, assigning the ETF a buy rating due to their high growth exposure and potential outperformance. Since, the ETF has beaten the S&P (SP500), up nearly 7% in comparison to 5.56% for the index.

Delivering Big Growth

As mentioned previously, Schwab's U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF is a simple, yet cost-effective way to achieve growth with exposure to AI. With the market expected to continue growing at a high rate, SCHG looks poised to benefit as a result of its high tech exposure.

Currently, SCHG's portfolio mostly consists of technology stocks with the sector making up 48%. What makes the fund unique is their passive investment style, meaning a low cost structure for long-term investors.

Like most ETFs, SCHG also rebalances its portfolio annually. This is a way to get exposure to outperforming companies and reducing exposure to underperforming ones.

Currently, Nvidia Corp (NVDA) is the only company in SCHG's portfolio that has double-digit exposure at 11.30%. The rest of the Magnificent 7 stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Broadcom (AVGO), and Amazon (AMZN) have high, single-digit exposure.

Broadcom and Nvidia have significantly outperformed the S&P (SP500), up over 92% and 33% respectively. Apple has been the biggest laggard, up single-digits, while Amazon has also been a bit of a disappointment in terms of price appreciation.

But this is the good thing about owning ETFs, especially growth-focused ones like SCHG. Another growth-focused fund I've been researching lately is Invesco's S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO).

A key difference is SPMO looks to track the S&P, while SCHG tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index. Another difference, something I give SPMO an advantage in is that the ETF rebalances twice a year instead of once.

I recently covered this in an article, so I won't go too much in-depth. But this potentially allows the fund to outperform vs other growth-focused peers like SCHG. The latter has a longer track record with an inception date of 12/11/2009 compared to 10/09/2015 for SPMO.

If you compare them from SPMO's inception date, SCHG still managed to slightly outperform SPMO in price returns, up nearly 384% compared to 375%.

Even if you compared to them to peers, who all came before them with the exception of Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SCHG still bested its peer group.

Using VOO's inception date of 09/07/2010, Schwab's U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF still outperformed. Vanguard's Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (MGK) came in a close second, up close to 861% to roughly 870% for SCHG.

In total returns, MGK slightly beats out SCHG, up 1,027.75% to 1,010.02%. VOO lags behind the entire group, up nearly 682%. All solid returns, but SCHG's long-term returns are attractive in comparison. Especially, for a passively-managed fund.

Additionally, SCHG has the lowest expense ratio of the group with the exception of VOO's 0.03%. VUG's is similar at 0.04%, while MGK has the highest of the group at 0.07%.

This, and SCHG's much cheaper share price makes them more appealing for individual investors. On a P/E basis, however, neither fund is cheap due to the market's overall valuation.

The chart below shows the price to earnings and price to book value ratio multiples of each fund from cheapest to most expensive. As you can see, neither is cheap relative to the overall market.

36.27x / 9.69x

SCHG is the third most expensive of the group. This also puts them at risk of underperformance if the market experiences a sharp pullback or correction. But seen as a growth holding, investors shouldn't be as deterred as they would with overly-expensive, individual stocks.

Their diversified portfolio also makes them more appealing despite their expensive appearance.

NAV Growth & Distributions

When it comes to investing in an ETF, NAV growth will determine their price and total return performance. Since inception, SCHG's NAV growth was solid, up 16.77%. Over the past 1, 3, 5, and 10 years this has also been higher, with higher NAV growth over the past 1 and 3 years.

I also like the fact that SCHG's distributions are mostly qualified, meaning lower taxes. As you can see below, their 2024 distributions were 99.99% qualified income. This also makes them favorable in taxable accounts, where I hold my largest position. I also own them in my Roth IRA as well.

For comparison, SPMO's distributions, which I touched on in the linked article above, are mostly ordinary income.

As a result, they are best held inside a tax-advantaged account. And this is something I give SCHG the edge over them despite their outperformance in the past year.

Investor Takeaway

Although SCHG appears expensive with a P/E of 36.27x, the fund should be held for foundational growth and their high tech exposure. This will likely provide capital appreciation and wealth building opportunities for the foreseeable future.

However, because of their performance and valuation, the fund could see a sharp selloff if the market crashes or experiences a steep correction. But if you're a long-term investor looking for a solid holding, SCHG's low-cost structure, diversified portfolio, and outperformance potential makes them a top pick.

data.title: Update On 2 Bank of America Preferreds: Rating Upgrade To Sell
data.description:
Introduction

In March of 2024, I rated two of the dozen preferred stocks issued by Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) as Strong Sells as better yield for similar risk were available from other banks. This review updates that analysis on the following issues:

  • Bank of America Corporation 5% NCUM PFD LL (NYSE:BAC.PR.N)
  • Bank of America Corporation 4.250% DP PFD QQ (NYSE:BAC.PR.Q).

At that time, both were tagged as Strong Sells due their low yields compared to other bank preferred stocks in yield. I won't be so hard this time, but the sub 6% yield still warrant a Sell rating, as better yields for similar risk are available from other major and regional banks.

Bank of America review

To assess the potential default risk, we need to review the financial statements of the issuer, who Seeking Alpha describes as such (edited):

Bank of America Corporation, through its subsidiaries, provides banking and financial products and services for individual consumers, small and middle-market businesses, institutional investors, large corporations, and governments worldwide. Its Consumer Banking segment offers traditional and money market savings accounts, certificates of deposit and IRAs, noninterest-and interest-bearing checking accounts, and investment accounts and products; and credit and debit cards, residential mortgages, and home equity loans, as well as direct and indirect loans, such as automotive, recreational vehicle, and consumer personal loans. The company's Global Wealth & Investment Management segment offers investment management, brokerage, banking, and trust and retirement products and services; and wealth management solutions, as well as customized solutions, including specialty asset management services. The bank was founded in 1784 and is headquartered in North Carolina.

The bank has almost 4000 branches spread across 39 states with California, Texas, and Florida being the largest concentrations.

Looking at the Income Statement, BAC even was profitable during the height of the COVID crisis.

Not shown but important: Net income is 20X the funds needed to pay all the preferred stock dividends, about $1.4B. Looking at the Balance Sheet to see how much Total Common Equity, or TCE, coverage is available to retire all the preferred stocks, we see a ratio of 11X, a very strong coverage level.

I'd say there is no risk of no dividend payments or loss if the bank failed. That said, if BAC did go under, the state of the U.S. economy most likely would be in shambles!

Comparing the preferreds

The next table compares the two preferred stocks.

Factor BAC-N BAC-Q
Issue Date 9/10/19 10/21/21
Issue size 52.4m shares 52m shares
Coupon 5.00% 4.25%
Call date 9/17/24 11/17/26
Price $21.49 $18.31
Yield 5.8% 5.8%
YTC NA 35%

Analysis

  • Both preferreds are rated BBB-, are non-cumulative, and qualify for the 15% tax rate.
  • Both issues currently provide the same yield.
  • Q's lower coupon but same yield should benefit investors holding these assets in taxable accounts.
  • BAC has several callable preferreds with high fixed coupons yet to be Called, which helps protect both issues, especially Q.
  • Q provides Call protection, whereas N does not.
  • While Q shows a 35% YTC, the odds of that happening are near zero in my opinion.
  • I think view these preferreds as "riskless" in terms of missing a dividend payment or being defaulted on. The damage to BAC would be too great.
  • Recent reviews (links listed later) show investors can get over 6% from Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), 9+% from offering from Citgroup (C).

Portfolio strategy

Since I hold almost all my preferred stocks in IRAs, the tax rate carries little weight to me. Whether the non-cumulative does depends on the importance of any dividends to the common stockholders, as they ceased getting paid if any of the preferred stocks stop paying. While just 2.3%, the BAC yield is in line with other major banks, thus I would be shocked if any dividend payments would be suspended, which didn’t happen during COVID or the Great Financial Crisis of 2008-09.

Key to me is the yield on these preferred stocks compared to similar issues with what I perceive to be equivalent nonpayment and/or default risks. That tradeoff has change to being more conservative as I have aged and now live with a positive cash flow in my retirement.

Final thoughts

Here are links to prior articles on Bank of America preferred stocks.

Bank of America Floating Preferred Comes With A 4% Floor.

First Look At 2 Bank of America Preferred Stocks (covers same ones reviewed here).

Here are other links for good alternatives to consider.

Huntington Bancshares: Evaluating Its Available Preferred Stocks.

Citigroup Capital XIII: High Yield Comes With Loss If Called - Still Rated A Buy.

data.title: Buy 9 Barron's Better Bets (Than T-Bills) From 15 'Safer' Of 23 October Dividend Dogs
data.description:
Foreword

While half of this collection of Barron's Better Bets (BBB) is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny dividends, nine of the fifteen highest yield Dogs with the "Safest" dividends of the BBB are ready to buy. October finds Conagra (CAG), Verizon (VZ), Altria Group (MO), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Truist Financial (TFC), UDR Inc (UDR), KeyCorp (KEY), Kimco Realty (KIM), and Regions Financial (RF), as the buyable top nine, living-up to the dogcatcher ideal. That is, each showed an annual dividend from $1K invested exceeding its single share price.

The remaining six of the October "Safer" fifteen have prices exceeding the dividend returned from $1k invested. Of the six, ONEOK (OKE) is closest to the mark with a price within $4.77 of equaling the dividend paid from $1k invested.

With renewed downside market pressure of 48.11%, it will be possible for all ten of the top BBB "Safer" dividend dogs to become elite fair-priced with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced October BBB Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the aforementioned nine as "safer" choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 20.73% To 38.12% Net Gains From Top-Ten BBB Dogs By October 2026

Five of ten top BBB Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top-ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this October, 2025 yield-based forecast for BBB dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to October 2026 were:

ONEOK (OKE) was projected to net $381.15 based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 1% over the market as a whole.

LyondellBasell (LYB) was projected to net $372.00, based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

Verizon was projected to net $271.89, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 64% under the market as a whole.

KeyCorp was projected to net $268.22, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% over the market as a whole.

Philip Morris International (PM) was projected to net $256.97, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% less than the market as a whole.

Bristol-Myers Squibb was projected to net $237.87, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% less than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $218.17, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 45% less than the market as a whole.

UDR Inc was projected to net $212.19, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

Regions Financial was projected to net $207.57, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% more than the market as a whole.

Equity Residential was projected to net $207.29 based on the median of target prices estimated by 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 26.33% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten BBB stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 24% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

October BBB Total Returns

October BBB Broker Targets

The October, 2025 BBB 23 By Yield

Actionable Conclusion: October Top Ten BBB Stocks By Yield Ranged 4.81% To 12.12% Per YCharts

Top ten October BBB Dogs occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the lone basic materials dog, LyondellBasell [1]. Then, two consumer staple stalwarts, placed second, and fifth, Conagra Brands [2], and Altria Group [5].

Thereafter, two health care sector members placed third and seventh, Pfizer [3], and Bristol-Myers Squibb [7].

A lone communication services sector placed fourth, Verizon [4]. Then, one energy stock placed sixth, ONEOK [6].

Two real estate sector dogs took the eighth, and ninth positions: Realty Income (O) [8], and Simon Property Group (SPG) [9].

Finally, another loner from the financial sector placed tenth, Truist, [10], and completed the October Dogs of the BBB by yield.

One of 23 October, 2025 BBB Placed in the Top Ten by Yield, Target, and Return

  • ONEOK Inc in the energy sector was the only October BBB member to place in the top ten in all three scoring categories.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows the relative strengths of the top ten October BBB dogs by dividend and price as of market close 10/21/2025.

BBB Prices per Dividend Dollar

The full chart above reveals twenty-three BBB dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock. The least expensive dog of the BBB is LyondellBasell at $8.25 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is Entergy at $39.86 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Six of Twenty-Three BBB Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Six BBB so infected are: LyondellBasell, Federal Realty, Kinder Morgan, Phillip Morris, Williams Companies, and Entergy. These are considered unsafe to buy. Furthermore their infection explains how the are missing from the IDEAL "safer" dividend dog reckoning.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, tariffs, trade-offs, war-games, rants, and uncertainty.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Dividend Splits Could Get All Ten BBB Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, seven in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those, three more top-yield stocks are in striking distance of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise or split their dividends. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 16.99% To 33.14% Top Ten BBB Upsides To October, 2025; (32) One -3.10% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 4.40% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 BBB Dogs As of October 21, 2026

Ten-top BBB dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten BBB dogs selected 10/21/25 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. (Consumer Cyclical, Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, Technology, and Utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield BBB Dogs (34) To Deliver 21.72% Vs. (35) 20.80% Net Gains by All Ten Come October 21, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten BBB Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 4.40% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The ninth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, ONEOK, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 38.12%.

The five lowest-priced BBB top-yield dogs for October were: Conagra; Pfizer; Verizon; Truist Financial; Bristol-Myers Squibb, with prices ranging from $18.62 to $44.54.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of October 21 were: LyondellBasell; Realty Income; Altria; ONEOK; Simon Property, with prices ranging from $45.21 to $178.72.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As summer cools and the market shivers, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and slithering presidential trade pronouncements add momentum toward 2026 (with the exception of a few overly speculative and meme stocks) look for all of the top ten to twenty-three BBB Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or real quick!

Afterword

Buy 9 of 15 'Safer' Dividend October Dogs of the BBB

Late-October 2025, 15 of the 23 BBB were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns with free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. Then nine IDEAL of the fifteen "safer" showed dividend yields from $1000 invested exceeding the price of a single share.

How BBB 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price" Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that seven dividends from $1K invested in top ten 'safer' dividend BBB dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of dividends from $1,000 invested being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those seven ideal dogs were Conagra, Verizon, Altria, Bristol-Myers Squibb, Truist, Regions Financial, UDR, and KeyCorp.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called "fair price."

A market correction of only 48.11% could put all ten share prices of these ten "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or make stock splits to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

BBB IDEAL "Safer" Dividends From Positive Return & Cash Flow Exceeding Dividend Yields

Adding the IDEAL dividends from stocks outside the top ten BBB list produces the above list of nine.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher BBB 23 Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" BBB dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.
